chapters.title: Introduction
chapters.description:
Hyperliquid is a sovereign Layer 1 blockchain purpose-built for on-chain finance — the first platform to run a fully on-chain Central Limit Order Book (CLOB) at institutional-grade throughput. Conceived by a founding team with roots in Hudson River Trading and quantitative finance, it was bootstrapped without venture capital and reached mainnet in early 2023.

Crypto has long forced a trade-off: the performance and UX of a centralized exchange, or the sovereignty and transparency of DeFi. Hyperliquid's thesis is that this trade-off is an artefact of bad architecture, not a law of nature. Rather than forcing trading primitives onto a general-purpose chain, it stacks three specialized layers: HyperBFT for consensus, HyperCore for perpetual and spot matching, and HyperEVM for Ethereum-compatible smart contracts. All three share one state and finalize in the same blocks — no bridges, no external oracles, no synchronization delay.

The result is a venue that has processed trillions in cumulative derivatives volume while remaining fully on-chain, transparent and self-custodial. The stated ambition is broader than crypto trading: to become the settlement layer for 24/7 global markets — equities, commodities, rates and prediction markets included. That is the "House of All Finance" thesis this wiki walks through, layer by layer.

chapters.title: HyperBFT
chapters.description:
HyperBFT is Hyperliquid's custom Byzantine Fault Tolerant consensus, derived from the HotStuff protocol originally designed for Meta's Libra project. It is engineered around a single non-negotiable: deliver the latency high-frequency trading needs without giving up the security guarantees institutions expect.

Four architectural choices make this possible. Pipelined consensus lets validators advance multiple block stages in parallel, collapsing median end-to-end latency to roughly 0.1–0.2 seconds. Optimistic execution runs transactions before finalization when network conditions allow. One-block finality makes every fill irreversible the moment it is included — no probabilistic settlement, no reorg risk, which is what lets a matching engine live on-chain at all. And classic BFT guarantees are preserved, tolerating up to one third of malicious validators.

Block production is weighted by native HYPE staked, so validator economics are tied directly to network security: a validator that misbehaves risks the stake that grants it the right to produce blocks. The stack sustains roughly 200,000 orders per second today, with architectural headroom well beyond 1M.

chapters.title: HyperCore
chapters.description:
HyperCore is Hyperliquid's financial execution engine — a Rust matching engine running fully on-chain, not a simulation and not an off-chain sequencer. Every order, cancel, trade, liquidation and funding payment is recorded on the public ledger under the same HyperBFT consensus that secures the rest of the network.

At its heart is an on-chain Central Limit Order Book with price-time priority — the same primitive used by NYSE, NASDAQ and the major CEXs, but settled natively on-chain. Execution is deterministic, so there is no black-box matching engine to trust. Order placement, cancellation and modification are gas-free for end users: the protocol's economic model absorbs the cost, removing the friction that historically made on-chain order books unusable.

Prices for perpetual markets come from a robust oracle: validators submit spot prices from multiple exchanges, and the median is used, making single-venue manipulation expensive. A cross-margin system lets traders use their whole portfolio as collateral across perps and spot at once. The engine handles ~200,000 orders per second — enough for market making, statistical arbitrage and delta-neutral strategies at institutional scale.

chapters.title: HyperEVM
chapters.description:
HyperEVM is Hyperliquid's general-purpose smart contract layer — Ethereum-compatible by design, so Solidity developers keep the tools, languages and mental models they already know. Critically, it is not a separate chain: HyperEVM and HyperCore are two execution environments secured by the same HyperBFT consensus, sharing a single state machine.

This unified architecture unlocks capabilities no L2 or rollup can match. Smart contracts can atomically read live HyperCore state — oracle prices, order book depth, positions, balances — and write back into it within a single block. No external oracle, no bridge, no cross-chain message queue, and therefore none of the failure modes those introduce.

To serve different workloads, HyperEVM runs a dual-block system: small blocks (2M gas, produced roughly every 2 seconds) for fast trades and near-instant confirmations, and big blocks (30M gas, roughly once per minute) for heavy contract deployments that would otherwise congest the network. Permissionless builders ship lending markets, vaults, options venues and structured products that plug straight into the native CLOB's liquidity rather than bootstrapping their own.

chapters.title: HyperCore ↔ HyperEVM
chapters.description:
Because HyperCore and HyperEVM share the same state under the same consensus, interactions between them are atomic — the property that separates Hyperliquid from every multi-chain or appchain stack.

Two system interfaces expose this composability. Read precompiles (starting at 0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000800) let HyperEVM contracts query real-time HyperCore data — oracle prices, perpetual positions, spot balances, order book depth — with zero external dependency. No Chainlink, no pull-oracle latency, no stale-price attack surface.

CoreWriter (the system contract at 0x3333333333333333333333333333333333333333) is the write path: contracts can place limit orders, manage vault transfers, handle staking or send any HyperCore action through a versioned ABI. Actions are delayed by a few seconds to neutralize latency games and keep the venue fair.

The payoff for builders is fully composable DeFi backed by a real on-chain CLOB: lending markets can liquidate directly on HyperCore, vaults can route trades without intermediaries, and structured products can settle against native spot and perp liquidity in a single transaction.

chapters.title: Trading & Risk
chapters.description:
Everything above is infrastructure; this is what you actually touch. Hyperliquid exposes a professional execution surface — the order types, margin modes and risk controls a derivatives trader expects — with the difference that every one of them is enforced on-chain by the protocol rather than by an exchange's internal database.

The three things worth understanding before sizing a position: how your order reaches the book, what backs it as collateral, and what happens when the market moves against you. The sub-chapters below cover each in turn.

subChapters.title: Order types
subChapters.subtitle: How your intent reaches the book
subChapters.description:
Hyperliquid supports the full professional order set. Market executes immediately at the best available price. Limit executes at your price or better and rests on the book if unmatched. Stop Market triggers a market order when the stop price is reached; Stop Limit places a limit order instead. Scale distributes multiple limit orders across a price range. TWAP splits a large order into 30-second sub-orders with a 3% maximum slippage cap, so size can be worked without printing it all at once.

Modifiers refine the behaviour. Good-Til-Cancel (GTC) rests until filled or cancelled. Immediate-Or-Cancel (IOC) cancels any unfilled portion instantly. Post-Only (ALO) guarantees you add liquidity and is rejected if it would take — the flag market makers live on, since it protects maker rebates. Reduce-Only can only shrink an existing position, which prevents an accidental reversal. Take-Profit and Stop-Loss attach automated exits at defined prices.

Because placement, cancellation and modification are gas-free, quote-heavy strategies that would be uneconomical on a general-purpose chain are viable here.

subChapters.title: Margin modes
subChapters.subtitle: Cross vs isolated, and what leverage really costs
subChapters.description:
Two margin modes cover different risk appetites. In cross margin, your entire account balance backs every open position: capital is used efficiently and a winning position can support a losing one, but a single bad trade draws on everything. In isolated margin, you allocate a fixed amount of collateral to one position — losses are capped at that allocation, and the rest of the account is untouched.

Leverage is capped per asset, not globally, and the caps are risk-tiered: deeper, more liquid markets allow more leverage than thin ones. Those caps are adjusted by the protocol when conditions demand it — after the March 2025 liquidation-arbitrage incident, maximum leverage was cut to 40x on Bitcoin and 25x on Ethereum, and a coefficient was applied to margin transfers.

Margin tiers matter as size grows: the larger the position, the lower the maximum leverage the system will allow, which limits the systemic damage any single account can inflict on the liquidation engine.

subChapters.title: Liquidations & ADL
subChapters.subtitle: What happens when the market moves against you
subChapters.description:
When a position's margin ratio falls below the maintenance requirement, it is liquidated on-chain. HLP — the protocol's liquidity vault — acts as the backstop, taking over the position and unwinding it. Liquidations are transparent and rule-based: there is no discretionary desk deciding who gets closed.

When a liquidation cannot be absorbed at an acceptable price — in a violent move where the book thins out — Auto-Deleveraging (ADL) is the last line of defence. Profitable traders on the opposite side of the market are partially closed at the bankruptcy price, so the system never carries bad debt. It is unpleasant if it happens to you, but it is what keeps the venue solvent without a socialized-loss fund.

These mechanisms have been stress-tested in public. In March 2025 a trader deliberately engineered their own liquidation to extract value from HLP, withdrawing unrealized profit to raise their liquidation price before the position was force-closed; HLP absorbed roughly $4M. The protocol responded within days by cutting leverage caps and constraining margin transfers — the adaptation loop is part of the design.

subChapters.title: Funding & fees
subChapters.subtitle: The recurring costs of holding a position
subChapters.description:
A perpetual future has no expiry, so something must tether it to spot: the funding rate. When perps trade above the underlying index, longs pay shorts; when they trade below, shorts pay longs. Funding accrues continuously and is settled on-chain, and it is the dominant cost of a position held for days rather than minutes — reading it before entering is not optional for a carry strategy.

Trading fees follow a maker-taker schedule that tightens with volume tiers, with rebates for the largest liquidity providers. Fees do not go to a company: the overwhelming majority flows into the Assistance Fund, which buys HYPE on the open market and burns it — meaning volume translates into token scarcity rather than corporate revenue.

Builder codes let any frontend, bot or interface attach an identifier to the orders it routes and earn a share of the fees those orders generate. It is how the ecosystem's mobile apps and terminals monetize without running their own matching engine or fragmenting liquidity.

chapters.title: Vaults & HLP
chapters.description: Not everyone wants to run a strategy. Vaults let capital be delegated to one that already works — and on Hyperliquid a vault is a protocol primitive, not a smart contract wrapped around an exchange API. Depositors share the profits and the losses of the strategy they back, with positions visible on-chain in real time.

subChapters.title: HLP
subChapters.subtitle: The protocol's own liquidity vault
subChapters.description:
HLP (Hyperliquidity Provider) is the community-owned vault that market-makes and, critically, absorbs liquidations as the counterparty of last resort. Anyone can deposit USDC into it and take a proportional share of the resulting PnL.

Its returns come from three streams: market-making spread, a share of trading fees, and the profit (or loss) of taking over liquidated positions. In calm markets that is a steady, boring yield. In a violent move it is genuinely risky — the March 2025 liquidation-arbitrage incident cost HLP roughly $4M — and that risk is the honest price of the yield. HLP is not a savings account.

What makes it unusual is the transparency: HLP's positions, PnL and drawdowns are on-chain and auditable at any moment, which is not true of the insurance funds CEXs run for the same purpose.

subChapters.title: User vaults
subChapters.subtitle: Strategies anyone can run or back
subChapters.description:
Beyond HLP, any trader can open a vault, run a strategy in it, and accept deposits. The vault leader trades the pooled capital; depositors hold a proportional claim on the results. The leader takes a share of the profits (10%) and must keep meaningful skin in the game by holding a portion of the vault themselves — so a leader cannot gamble purely with other people's money.

Because the vault lives on HyperCore rather than in an off-chain account, depositors can verify every position and every fill. There is no monthly PDF statement to trust: the strategy is legible while it runs.

The honest caveat is that transparency is not protection. A visible strategy can still be a bad one, past performance is not a guarantee, and a vault concentrated in one leveraged direction can lose most of its value in a single session. Read the positions, not just the return curve.

chapters.title: Spot & Unit
chapters.description: Perpetuals made Hyperliquid's name, but a settlement layer for all finance has to hold real assets too. Two mechanisms cover that: a permissionless spot market where tokens are deployed and listed by auction, and Unit, the tokenization layer that brings external assets like Bitcoin and Ethereum onto the chain natively.

subChapters.title: Spot markets & deploy auctions
subChapters.subtitle: How a token gets listed without a listing desk
subChapters.description:
Spot markets on Hyperliquid run on the same CLOB as perps, with the same price-time priority and the same on-chain settlement. What differs is how a market comes into existence: there is no listing committee and no business-development deal.

Instead, a spot slot is won through a 31-hour Dutch auction, paid in HYPE. The price starts high and decays until someone takes it, so access is priced by demand rather than granted by relationship — and the cost is high enough to make spam uneconomical. The winner registers the token's name, decimals and total supply at the protocol level (see HIP-1) and distributes a genesis allocation.

The auction price is itself a useful ecosystem signal: it tracks how much builders are willing to pay for distribution on Hyperliquid at a given moment.

subChapters.title: Unit
subChapters.subtitle: Bringing BTC, ETH and SOL on-chain natively
subChapters.description:
HyperCore matches orders and HyperEVM runs contracts, but neither by itself turns a USDC-denominated venue into a multi-asset one. That is Unit's role: a decentralized tokenization protocol built specifically for Hyperliquid that lets users deposit major assets — Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana — and trade them as native spot on the CLOB.

Unit works on a lock-and-mint model operated by a guardian network: the underlying asset is locked on its home chain, and a corresponding native asset is minted on Hyperliquid, redeemable in the other direction. The point is that the resulting asset is a first-class citizen of the order book rather than a wrapped IOU sitting in an AMM pool.

This is what allows a spot BTC market to exist on a chain that is not Bitcoin, with the same execution quality as any other pair — and it is the plumbing behind the broader move toward equities, commodities and RWAs settling on Hyperliquid.

chapters.title: Stablecoins & USDH
chapters.description: Every derivatives venue is ultimately denominated in something. Hyperliquid's answer evolved from simply using USDC to creating a protocol-native stablecoin standard whose economics flow back to the network rather than to an external issuer.

subChapters.title: USDH
subChapters.subtitle: The native stablecoin, decided by governance
subChapters.description:
USDH is Hyperliquid's native stablecoin ticker, and the way it was assigned is as interesting as the asset itself. Rather than partnering privately with an issuer, the protocol put the ticker to a validator governance vote, with competing proposals from established stablecoin players and newcomers.

Native Markets won. The winning proposal committed to routing reserve yield back into the Hyperliquid ecosystem rather than retaining it as issuer profit — the decisive argument, since it converts a cost centre (collateral sitting in an issuer's T-bills) into value that returns to the network.

USDH now serves as settlement collateral across the ecosystem, notably for several HIP-3 deployers who chose it over USDC or USDT for their markets.

subChapters.title: Aligned Quote Assets
subChapters.subtitle: Making stablecoin issuers pay rent to the network
subChapters.description:
Aligned Quote Assets (AQA) generalizes the USDH idea into a standing primitive. A stablecoin issuer that wants its asset to be a first-class quote currency on Hyperliquid stakes 1,000,000 HYPE and, in exchange, earns protocol-level fee advantages for markets denominated in it.

The effect is a competitive market between issuers — USDC, USDT0, USDH, USDe and others all circulate as collateral in different corners of the ecosystem — where the privilege of being quote currency is bought with an aligned, locked stake rather than granted by a listing agreement.

For traders this means collateral choice is now a real decision: different HIP-3 markets settle in different stablecoins, and some collateral (like yield-bearing USDe) earns while it backs a position.

chapters.title: $HYPE
chapters.description:
HYPE is the native token of Hyperliquid — the coordination asset that aligns validators, traders and builders. It launched on November 29, 2024 through one of the largest community-first genesis distributions in DeFi history, with no venture allocation and no pre-sale.

Its utilities sit at the base of the stack: gas for HyperEVM, the staking asset that weights block production on HyperBFT, the currency of governance actions, and the collateral large-scale applications must lock — 500,000 HYPE to deploy a HIP-3 market, 1,000,000 HYPE to issue an Aligned Quote Asset.

subChapters.title: Distribution
subChapters.subtitle: Who holds the supply, and on what schedule
subChapters.description:
Total supply is capped at 1 billion HYPE. The allocation is deliberately community-weighted: 31% went to the genesis airdrop, 38.9% is reserved for future emissions and community rewards, 23.8% to core contributors, 6% to the Hyper Foundation and 0.3% to grants.

That puts roughly 70% of supply on the community side of the ledger, against an industry norm closer to 15–20% — which is the concrete expression of having taken no venture money. There was no cap table to satisfy at launch.

Contributor and foundation allocations unlock on a schedule running through 2027, so supply overhang is a real variable to track rather than a solved problem: the burn described in the next sub-chapter is what offsets it.

subChapters.title: Assistance Fund & burn
subChapters.subtitle: How fees turn into scarcity
subChapters.description:
The Assistance Fund began as an insurance backstop and was transformed by governance into the protocol's deflationary engine. The mechanism is deliberately simple: up to 99% of net protocol fees flow into the fund, the fund continuously buys HYPE on the open market, and the acquired tokens are permanently burned.

The consequence is that circulating supply moves with real usage. Net supply change is emissions minus the burn rate — and at current revenue levels (roughly $1B annualized) the burn materially exceeds emissions, making the token net deflationary while volume holds.

This is the sharpest difference from an inflationary rewards model: HYPE accrues value from fees people actually paid to trade, not from printing new tokens to pay for liquidity. The corollary is equally true — if volume falls, so does the burn.

subChapters.title: Staking & liquid staking
subChapters.subtitle: Securing the chain without idling your capital
subChapters.description:
Staking HYPE delegates it to a validator and weights that validator's right to produce blocks, earning a share of rewards in return. It is the mechanism that makes attacking consensus expensive: an attacker needs stake, and misbehaviour puts that stake at risk.

The trade-off is the usual one — staked capital is locked and unavailable as collateral. Liquid staking protocols close that gap by issuing a transferable receipt token (Kinetiq's kHYPE being the most established) that represents the staked position and can then be used across HyperEVM DeFi as collateral, in lending markets or in liquidity pools.

The convenience carries added risk: you now hold a claim on staked HYPE via a smart contract rather than the stake itself, which adds contract risk and the possibility that the receipt trades below its underlying in stressed markets.

chapters.title: HIP Framework
chapters.description: Hyperliquid Improvement Proposals (HIPs) are the binding code upgrades that extend the protocol. Each HIP introduces a new primitive — token standards, liquidity bootstrapping, permissionless market creation, outcome trading — that the whole ecosystem then builds on. Read in order, they are the clearest narrative of how Hyperliquid went from a perps exchange to general market infrastructure.

subChapters.title: HIP-1
subChapters.subtitle: Native token standard
subChapters.description:
HIP-1 introduced Hyperliquid's foundational token standard, turning the platform from a perpetuals-only exchange into generalized asset infrastructure. Any entity can deploy a native HIP-1 token permissionlessly by winning a 31-hour Dutch auction for a spot slot — a mechanism that prices market access fairly and prevents spam, with the auction fee paid in HYPE.

At deployment, a token's name, decimals and total supply are registered at the protocol level and distributed through a genesis allocation to anchor holders. HIP-1 tokens can then be linked to ERC-20 contracts on HyperEVM, so the same asset moves between the native CLOB and the smart contract layer without wrapping or bridging.

Integrations with LayerZero, Chainlink CCIP and Wormhole extend HIP-1 tokens across the broader multi-chain ecosystem — but the canonical supply always lives on Hyperliquid, where the order book, the gas and the governance logic coexist.

subChapters.title: HIP-2
subChapters.subtitle: Hyperliquidity
subChapters.description:
HIP-2 solved the cold-start problem for new token markets by embedding automated liquidity provision directly into block transition logic — not as an operator-controlled vault, but as a consensus-secured market-making primitive called Hyperliquidity.

The strategy guarantees a 0.3% spread refreshed every three seconds from the first second a token is live, creating tradable liquidity without a mercenary market maker. Because quotes are produced by the protocol itself, they cannot be withdrawn, rugged or selectively disabled the moment conditions get difficult.

Crucially, Hyperliquidity quotes into the general-purpose order book rather than a segregated pool. Professional market makers can therefore join alongside it as demand grows — a clean hand-off from protocol-native bootstrapping to organic, competitive liquidity.

subChapters.title: HIP-3
subChapters.subtitle: Builder-deployed perpetuals
subChapters.description:
HIP-3, live since October 2025, turned the platform from a curated exchange into permissionless perpetual-futures infrastructure — the most significant decentralization milestone in Hyperliquid's history.

Any builder staking 500,000 HYPE can deploy a perpetual market for any asset — crypto, equities, commodities, rates — and capture 50% of the trading fees it generates. The protocol supplies the matching engine, risk engine, margin system and liquidation flow; the builder supplies the oracle, the product thesis and the distribution. Validators can slash 20–100% of a deployer's stake for technical failure or oracle manipulation, and slashed HYPE is burned rather than redistributed.

The result is a competitive market of market operators. TradeXYZ dominates by volume with equities, indices and commodities; Dreamcash settles RWAs in USDT0; Felix and Ventuals build on USDH, the latter listing pre-IPO names like SpaceX and OpenAI; HyENA runs crypto majors on yield-bearing USDe collateral. Note the pattern: each deployer picks its own collateral, which is why stablecoin choice became a strategic question.

subChapters.title: HIP-4
subChapters.subtitle: Outcome markets
subChapters.description:
HIP-4 adds the fourth primitive: outcome trading. Where HIP-3 let builders deploy perpetuals on any price, HIP-4 lets them deploy markets on any resolvable question — an election, an approval, a shipping date — settled on the same engine, order book and margin system as everything else.

Mechanically an outcome market trades tokens that redeem at a fixed value if the event occurs and zero if it does not, so the price is a live probability. Because it runs on HyperCore rather than a bespoke prediction-market venue, it inherits the CLOB, the risk engine and the composability with HyperEVM — a lending market or a vault can take a position in an outcome the same way it would take one in a perp.

It is the piece that stretches "House of All Finance" past price speculation into information markets. It is also the newest and least battle-tested of the four primitives, so treat liquidity and resolution rules as things to check per market rather than assume.
